title: Supplier Bona Fides and the Falsified Medicines Directive
author: B. Subramanian
excerpt: How UK and EU quality teams verify supplier bona fides under the Falsified Medicines Directive and GDP, with practical checks, red flags and QMS controls.
cover_image_prompt: A photorealistic clinical-luxury pharmaceutical banner in a cool teal and cyan palette over deep navy, with a single subtle green accent. Soft natural light falls across a polished laboratory bench where a gloved hand holds a sealed medicine carton bearing an unmarked 2D data-matrix and an intact anti-tampering seal, while a slightly out-of-focus tablet screen suggests a verification register in the background. Shallow depth of field draws the eye to the carton and seal; cool reflective surfaces, fine condensation and clean shadows convey precision and trust. Composition is 4:3, elegant and uncluttered, with no text, no logos and no readable characters anywhere in the frame.

faqs.q: What does 'supplier bona fides' mean in a pharmaceutical context?
faqs.a: It refers to documented verification that a supplier, broker or trader genuinely is who they claim to be and holds the correct authorisation to supply the medicines in question. In practice this means confirming licences, registered identity and scope against the regulator's own records before any transaction. It is a core Good Distribution Practice control and a frontline defence against falsified medicines entering the legitimate supply chain.

faqs.q: How does the Falsified Medicines Directive affect UK suppliers after Brexit?
faqs.a: The FMD safety features and decommissioning continue to apply in Northern Ireland under the EU framework, while Great Britain operates its own arrangements following EU exit. Quality teams must therefore apply the regime appropriate to each leg of a transaction rather than a single blanket procedure. Checking current MHRA guidance is essential, as the GB and NI positions differ and continue to evolve.

faqs.q: How often should supplier bona fides be re-verified?
faqs.a: Verification should follow a risk-based cycle rather than a one-off check at onboarding, because authorisations can be varied, suspended or revoked at any time. Higher-risk suppliers, brokers and new trading relationships warrant more frequent revalidation. The schedule and triggers should be defined in your quality management system so that regulatory action against a counterparty is detected before product changes hands.
